original_hypothesis.title: ASDUSDC decimal underflow/overflow risk when whitelisting tokens with decimals > 18 can brick deposit/withdraw/recover for that token
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ASDUSDC.deposit/withdraw/recover compute scaling with (this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals()). If a whitelisted token reports decimals > 18, the subtraction underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8, permanently breaking deposit/withdraw/recover for that version and potentially trapping that token if it is transferred in.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic operational/config hazard given the contract is designed to wrap multiple "USDC versions" across chains, where token metadata can vary or be malicious. The code trusts external decimals().
deployment_assumptions: Owner whitelists a token with decimals() > 18, or a token whose decimals() can change over time
Users or router interact with that token version
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No on-chain check in updateWhitelist() to restrict decimals <= 18. deposit/withdraw/recover all call ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals() directly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: 10 ** (this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals()) used in multiple functions; subtraction can underflow if token decimals > 18

preconditions: ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals() returns a value > 18 (or reverts unexpectedly)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: revert bricks flows and can lock funds
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No guard in updateWhitelist or per-call require(decimals<=18)

analysis_questions: What tokens are intended to be whitelisted and what are their decimals across supported chains?
Could any bridged representation report unexpected decimals or revert on decimals()?
Is decimals() guaranteed constant for all whitelisted tokens?
validation_requirements: Static check/registry of allowed token addresses with verified decimals
Unit tests with decimals=19+ token to confirm revert behavior and user impact
Consider caching decimals per version at whitelist time to avoid dynamic behavior

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a token with decimals=20 is whitelisted, any deposit/withdraw/recover involving it reverts, potentially trapping user funds in that token version if transferred directly or partially processed by integrations.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate; can lock funds for a specific version and disrupt router swaps
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users holding that version or asdUSDC minted from it
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Whitelist correction may not unlock if withdraw also requires whitelist true; migration would be needed

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for decimals edge cases

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Reverts with arithmetic underflow in scaling computation

validation_result.impact_summary: If a token with decimals > 18 is whitelisted, deposit/withdraw/recover all revert due to arithmetic underflow, permanently bricking those functions for that token version and potentially trapping any tokens of that version sent to the contract.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ASDUSDC contract computes decimal scaling in deposit(), withdraw(), and recover() using the expression `this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals()`. Since `this.decimals()` is hardcoded to 18 (OpenZeppelin ERC20 default), if any whitelisted token reports decimals > 18, this subtraction underflows. In Solidity 0.8.22 (the compiler version used), unsigned integer underflow causes an automatic revert. The updateWhitelist() function has no guard to check that the token's decimals are <= 18, meaning the owner can inadvertently whitelist a problematic token. Once whitelisted, any call to deposit(), withdraw(), or recover() for that token version will revert. If tokens of that version are sent directly to the contract (e.g., by mistake or via the router), they become permanently trapped since recover() also uses the same underflowing math.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerable pattern is present in three separate functions in asdUSDC.sol. The `updateWhitelist` function performs no validation on the token's decimals. The subtraction `this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals()` operates on uint8 values (ERC20.decimals() returns uint8), and Solidity 0.8's checked arithmetic will revert on underflow.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: If ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals() > 18 (this.decimals()), the subtraction underflows in Solidity 0.8, reverting the transaction. No guard prevents whitelisting such a token.

Same underflow issue as deposit. Users who somehow hold asdUSDC minted from another version cannot withdraw to this version (though this is less impactful than the deposit/recover case). More critically, if the version was previously valid and later changed its decimals() return value, existing depositors are locked out.
The recovery function also uses the same vulnerable pattern. If tokens with decimals > 18 are sent directly to the contract, recover() cannot rescue them since it also reverts on the underflow.
No validation that ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals() <= this.decimals(). This is the missing safeguard that would prevent the underflow condition.

calling_sequence: 1. Owner calls updateWhitelist(tokenWithDecimals20, true) - no decimals check, succeeds
4. SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom transfers tokens to the contract
7. Computation: 18 - 20 underflows uint8, Solidity 0.8 reverts with panic(0x11)
8. Entire transaction reverts - tokens are not transferred (safeTransferFrom also reverted)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path starts at updateWhitelist() which lacks a `require(ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals() <= this.decimals())` check. Once a >18-decimal token is whitelisted, any call to deposit/withdraw/recover that references it hits the underflow at the decimal scaling computation. Since the revert happens after the whitelist check but during the arithmetic, the function is completely bricked for that token version. For recover(), tokens already in the contract cannot be rescued since recover() also uses the same underflowing computation.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Owner whitelists a token with decimals() > 18
Users or router interact with that token version
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployASDRouter.js script whitelists specific addresses (0x59a49a3646eC96339525B89bEB35c1c095cc1992 on canto, 0xa5bFA7E06eBc71Ca3cc58f5AC0954ebf6E9890E5 on canto-testnet) which are presumably standard USDC variants with 6 decimals. However, updateWhitelist() is a runtime function callable by the owner at any time - the deployment script only sets the initial whitelist. No deployment-time or runtime validation of token decimals exists.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is realistic for an operational/configuration error: (1) A new chain's bridged USDC representation uses non-standard decimals (e.g., 20 or 24), or a malicious token is accidentally whitelisted; (2) Owner calls updateWhitelist(badToken, true) with no error because there's no decimals validation; (3) Any subsequent deposit/withdraw/recover call for that token version reverts with arithmetic underflow; (4) If tokens were sent directly to the contract before the issue is discovered, they are trapped since recover() also reverts. While the attack requires owner misconfiguration, the code defect is the missing validation - a defensive programming failure.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The defect requires owner misconfiguration to trigger, but once triggered: (1) deposit/withdraw/recover are permanently bricked for the affected token version; (2) tokens sent directly to the contract for that version are unrecoverable; (3) if the ASDRouter interacts with the whitelisted version, the deposit call reverts, causing lzCompose to fail at the deposit step and trigger refunds (which may themselves fail per other identified issues). The owner can mitigate by de-whitelisting the token, but any tokens already in the contract remain trapped.
recommended_mitigations: Add a decimals check in updateWhitelist: require(ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals() <= decimals(), 'ASDUSDC: token decimals too high');
Cache the decimals value at whitelist time to prevent dynamic changes: mapping(address => uint8) public usdcDecimals; set during updateWhitelist and use cached value in deposit/withdraw/recover
Add a require(_amount > 0) check in deposit/withdraw to prevent zero-amount edge cases
Consider using a safe subtraction pattern with an explicit check before the exponentiation
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC20 token with decimals() returning 20. 2. Deploy ASDUSDC. 3. Call updateWhitelist(mockToken, true) - expect success. 4. Approve ASDUSDC to spend mockToken. 5. Call deposit(mockToken, 1000) - expect revert with Panic(0x11) arithmetic underflow. 6. Send mockToken directly to ASDUSDC contract. 7. Call recover(mockToken) as owner - expect revert with same panic. 8. Confirm tokens are trapped.
uncertainties: The exact addresses whitelisted in production and their decimals are not fully verifiable from deployment scripts alone
Whether the owner would realistically whitelist a >18 decimal token depends on operational procedures outside the code
A token with mutable decimals() (extremely rare but theoretically possible) could change after whitelisting
additional_testing_needed: Unit test with a mock token returning decimals() = 19, 20, 255 to confirm revert behavior
Integration test verifying ASDRouter behavior when deposit reverts due to this issue
Fuzz test on updateWhitelist + deposit with random decimals values
